hints.accusative: Accusative feminine would be 'Magnam' — 'Magna' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'Magna' too, but here it modifies nominative 'gratia'
hints.vocative: 'Magna' is not being addressed — it modifies the subject 'gratia'

steps.reveal: dis immortalibus = to the immortal gods (dat.)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'di immortales' — 'dis immortalibus' is dative or ablative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'deos immortales' — 'dis immortalibus' is dative
hints.ablative: 'Dis immortalibus' could be ablative, but with 'habenda est gratia' it is dative of recipient

hints.2nd declension (regular): 'Deus' is 2nd declension but has irregular plurals: di/dii, deorum, dis/diis, deos, dis/diis
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'dei' ends in -i (2nd declension)
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'dei' ends in -i

steps.reveal: habenda est = must be held / given (gerundive obligation)

hints.future passive indicative: Future passive would be 'habebitur' — 'habenda' is a gerundive expressing obligation
hints.perfect passive indicative: Perfect passive would be 'habita est' — 'habenda' with -nd- is a gerundive
hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'habetur' — 'habenda est' expresses obligation

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'habenda est' translates to?

hints.to dwell: 'To dwell' is 'habitare' — 'habere' means to have or hold
hints.to give: 'To give' is 'dare' — 'habere' means to have; 'gratiam habere' = to feel gratitude
hints.to consider: 'Habere' can mean 'to consider,' but with 'gratiam' it means to feel/express thanks

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell) — different verb
hints.abeo, abire, abii, abitum: These are the parts of 'abire' (to go away)
hints.debeo, debere, debui, debitum: These are the parts of 'debere' (to owe) — different verb

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'gratiam' — 'gratia' here is nominative singular, subject of 'habenda est'
hints.ablative: Ablative would also be 'gratia,' but here it is the subject of the passive periphrastic
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'gratia' — but thanks are not being addressed here

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gratiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'gratiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'gratiae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What does the idiom 'gratiam habere' mean?

hints.to hold a favor: 'Gratiam referre' means to return a favor — 'gratiam habere' is to feel thanks
hints.to have grace: 'Gratia' here means thanks, not grace — the idiom means to feel gratitude
hints.to possess charm: 'Gratia' can mean charm, but with 'habere' it is the idiom for expressing gratitude

steps.prompt: In the passive periphrastic, who is the implied agent?
steps.target: the speaker and citizens (understood from context)
choices: the speaker and citizens (understood from context)

hints.the immortal gods: The gods are the recipients (dative) of gratitude, not the agents who must give it
hints.Catiline: Catiline is the enemy — he would not be expressing gratitude to the gods
hints.the senate: While the senate is present, the dative of agent is omitted, leaving the obligation general

steps.prompt: What is the word order effect of separating 'Magna...gratia' across the sentence?
steps.target: hyperbaton: creates emphasis by framing the obligation within the modifier
choices: hyperbaton: creates emphasis by framing the obligation within the modifier

hints.anaphora: repetition at the start: Anaphora repeats words at the start of clauses — this is separation of adjective from noun
hints.chiasmus: mirror-image structure: Chiasmus is ABBA order — this is hyperbaton (splitting 'Magna...gratia')
hints.asyndeton: omission of conjunctions: Asyndeton omits conjunctions — here the figure is the wide separation of adjective and noun

translation: Great gratitude must be given to the immortal gods
